does: This paper introduces MemGym, a comprehensive benchmark for evaluating memory capabilities in long-horizon LLM agent tasks. The benchmark unifies multiple existing agent evaluation environments under a single memory-reasoning interface, spanning five evaluation tracks across four distinct agentic regimes: tool-use dialogue, multi-turn deep research, coding tasks, and computer use. A key methodological contribution is the introduction of memory-isolated scoring that decouples memory performance from confounding factors like reasoning ability, retrieval capability, and tool-use proficiency. The benchmark includes synthetic data pipelines for code question-answering and deep research tasks that are length-controllable and thoroughly ablated at each stage, enabling more precise attribution of performance differences to memory mechanisms rather than other agent components.
connects: This work builds on recent efforts to evaluate LLM agents in realistic execution scenarios, extending beyond earlier memory benchmarks that focused narrowly on personalized information retention in chat settings. It synthesizes insights from multiple established agent evaluation frameworks including SWE-Gym for software engineering tasks and WebArena for web navigation. The paper responds to a recognized gap in the literature: prior memory benchmarks fail to capture the dynamic memory formation and update patterns required during extended agentic execution. It connects to broader research on long-context modeling and retrieval-augmented generation, while also drawing on methodology from causal inference and ablation studies to isolate memory performance from confounding factors.
enables: MemGym establishes infrastructure for systematic investigation of memory strategies in realistic agent settings, enabling future work to develop and compare more sophisticated memory mechanisms without confounds. The memory-isolated scoring framework allows researchers to identify which memory architectures truly improve agent performance versus appearing beneficial due to correlated improvements in reasoning or retrieval. The length-controllable synthetic data pipelines support controlled experiments examining how memory systems scale with task complexity and information density. This work points toward developing specialized memory mechanisms tailored to different agentic regimes, understanding memory update dynamics across extended execution, and designing more efficient memory retrieval strategies that balance recall accuracy against computational cost in practical deployment scenarios.
